segments.text: Welcome back to the MCP workshop series.
Last time we covered the data pillar, understanding what types of data exist and how to evaluate
their quality.
Now, it's time to talk about what we can actually do with that data.
This is the second pillar, artificial intelligence.
Before we go any further, let's clear up a confusion that trips up almost everyone.
What's the difference between an algorithm and AI?
An algorithm follows a fixed series of steps to solve a task.
It always behaves the same way when provided with the same inputs.
Think of it like a recipe.
Same ingredients, same steps, same results.
Artificial intelligence is different.
AI uses data to improve over time.
It can adapt to new situations and make decisions without being explicitly programmed for each
one.
That's the fundamental shift.
A calculator follows rules.
An AI system learns patterns from data and makes decisions based on what it's seen before.
Now AI isn't one technology.
It's a family of techniques.
And the useful way to understand them is through the lens of human capabilities.
Humans can see here and speak, understand language, act in the physical world, learn from
experience, find relevant information, and reason through complex problems.
AI mirrors each of these capabilities with a corresponding technology.
Let's walk through them one by one.
And for each one, I'll give you concrete use cases from our industry.
The ability to see maps to computer vision.
Interview vision interprets images and 3D environments to extract useful information and detect
patterns.
The input data can be images, video, or 3D geometry.
The goal is information extraction, pattern detection, and object recognition.
In AC, this enables automated quality control, where computer vision analyzes 3Db models,
and site photos to detect deviations and ensure compliance.
It also enables object recognition and classification, where AI identifies construction elements like
doors, windows, and pipes in a beam model or a site scan.
The ability to hear and speak maps to STT, speech to text, and TTS, text to speech technologies.
These enable voice synthesis and transcription, converting text to speech and vice versa.
In AC, speech to text enables automated beam documentation, where spoken notes from
inspectors and architects are transcribed directly into beam data, reducing manual input.
TTS and STT together power virtual beam assistants that can answer questions about materials, compliance,
and model data through natural conversation.
Understanding language maps to natural language processing, or NLP.
NLP enables interpretation, analysis, and extraction of information from text data.
The goal is to extract meaning, behavior, and emotion from written content.
This powers automated compliance checking, where NLP analyzes regulations and compares them
to beam models to flag non-compliance.
It also enables issue tracking and reporting, where the model processes incident reports,
emails, chats, and field notes, and link them to beam elements for faster resolution.
The ability to act maps to robotics and automation.
This covers automating repetitive or complex tasks using intelligent systems and robots.
The goal is to perform physical and virtual tasks and make decisions.
In AC, this means automated prefabrication and assembly, where robots use beam data to
cut, assemble, and transport components, reducing wastes and boosting efficiency.
It also means AI powered side inspections, where drones and robots navigate sites, compare
progress to beam models, and detect real-time discrepancies.
The ability to learn maps to machine learning.
Machine learning uses historical data to identify patterns and generate predictive models.
The goal is pattern recognition, outcome prediction, and rule discovery.
This enables construction risk assessment, where AI analyzes historical data and beam
models to predict delays, cost of a run, and safety risks.
It also enables AI-based cost estimation, where machine learning generates accurate cost
estimates, from beam models, reducing budget uncertainty.
The ability to find relevant information maps to RIG, or retrieval augmented generation.
RIG combines information retrieval and content generation to deliver accurate contextual
responses.
The goal is quick retrieval of relevant information.
This powers automated specification matching, where the model compares manufacturer databases
with beam requirements to recommend optimal materials.
It also enables smart issue resolution, where AI analyzes past issues and suggests contextual
solutions within the beam environment.
Finally, the ability to reason maps to logical inference.
Logical inference applies rules to make informed decisions based on available data.
This powers design conflict resolution, where AI analyzes conflicting requirements, for
example, structure versus energy efficiency, and suggests optimal solutions.
It also enables layout optimization, where the model recommends optimal spatial layouts
based on functional and regulatory requirements.
So that's the AI pillar.
Seven human capabilities, seven AI technologies, each with real applications in our industry.
The key takeaway is that AI isn't magic.
It's a set of tools, each designed for a specific type of problem, and the better your data,
the more powerful these tools become.
Next time, we move to the third pillar, APIs.
We look at how systems connect to each other, how data flows between platforms, and why
traditional APIs have a fundamental limitation when it comes to AI.
Leave your questions in the comments, and I'll see you for the next one.
